data.title: Fixed-Income Outlook: From Oil Shock To Oil Spillover?
data.description:
Oil’s rise could linger. Here are six ways bond investors can build resilience.

Geopolitics rarely stay contained to the headlines for long. Conflict in the Middle East is already reverberating through energy markets, reminding investors how quickly war can ripple into the global economy. Oil prices are the key transmission channel, and how far they rise and how long they remain elevated could shape the outlook for growth, inflation and policy, with implications for bond markets.

War's Transmission Pipeline: Oil

The war in Iran is the latest in a series of shocks with the potential to disrupt economies and financial markets. If the conflict resolves quickly and energy prices retreat, the shock may prove short-lived. If not, it could be much more severe. The only certainty now is uncertainty.

West Texas Intermediate crude traded around $67 a barrel on February 27, the day before hostilities escalated. Within 10 days, prices had surged past $100. But what matters most for the global economy is persistence. Higher fuel costs force governments, households and businesses to spend more on energy and less elsewhere - raising the risk of a stagflationary mix of slower growth and higher prices.

For central banks, that combination is tricky. Should central bankers raise rates to rein in rising prices? Or lower rates to stem slowing growth? The quandary suggests that the Federal Reserve and European Central Bank may stay on hold as they await further clarity. And while China has accumulated an oil stockpile that should see it through the supply disruption for now, other Asian economies are major importers of oil products, and most don’t have the ability to replace oil and gas that typically come from the Middle East.

Is This 2022 All Over Again?

Today’s oil shock invites comparisons to 2022, when Russia’s invasion of Ukraine triggered a sharp surge in energy prices, reigniting inflation and forcing central banks to tighten aggressively. The result was the worst year on record for modern bond markets, which tumbled in tandem with equities. But the backdrop today is different.

Policy rates are no longer near zero, there is no post-pandemic reopening tailwind, and fiscal capacity is more constrained. That leaves central banks facing a more difficult trade-off: tightening into a supply-driven shock risks exacerbating an already fragile macro environment and increasing the risk of financial stress, while holding back allows inflation pressures to build.

This is not 2022 - and, in our view, reacting as if it were would be a mistake. So far, financial markets have been volatile but well-behaved. But geopolitical conflicts are complex and unpredictable. For bond markets, the result is greater uncertainty around the path of inflation and interest rates - conditions that call for caution but that also widen dispersion across regions and sectors, creating opportunities for active investors.

Six Strategies to Put into Action

Our outlook entering the year favored resilient portfolios - active duration, global diversification, and a balance of rate and credit risks. In our view, these strategies remain well-suited to today’s increasing uncertainty, as they help position active investors to absorb volatility and capture new opportunities as they arise.

Diversify duration. In our view, today’s environment argues for keeping bonds anchored within overall portfolios - and that means holding duration. But don’t just set your portfolio duration and forget it. When yields are higher (and bond prices lower), lengthen the duration; when yields are lower (and prices higher), trim your sails. And remember, high current yields provide a cushion against price declines.

We believe that duration should also be sourced from diverse regions. A globally diversified approach to duration may offer a sturdier foundation for bond portfolios. Curve positioning, too, is a lever that shapes how portfolios respond as the rate environment evolves.

Government bonds remain the purest source of interest rate sensitivity and remain essential for liquidity. But investors can also take duration through securitized markets such as agency mortgage-backed securities, which provide both duration and incremental yield. Securitized assets are also somewhat less exposed to energy-related disruption, in our analysis.

Adopt a balanced stance. As we see it, a balanced posture across rates and credit provides a sturdier mix of resilience and income. Among the most effective strategies are those that pair government bonds and other interest rate-sensitive assets with growth-oriented credit assets in a single, dynamically managed portfolio.

This pairing helps diversify exposure to macro drivers and mitigate tail risks. Combining diversifying assets in a single portfolio makes it easier to manage the interplay of rate and credit risks and to readily tilt toward duration or credit according to changing market conditions.

Focus on quality credit. Since the conflict escalated, credit spreads have risen modestly off extremely tight levels but remain contained. Credit has repriced less sharply than stocks, and we expect that resilience to persist. We think yield levels are a more reliable guide to forward returns than spreads alone. And yields look compelling across many credit-sensitive sectors.

That said, the range of potential outcomes has widened, making selectivity key, in our view. Structural themes such as the AI-driven capex cycle continue to create opportunity, though we believe investors should approach them with cautious optimism, looking past extremes of enthusiasm and aversion. Recent geopolitical developments are also introducing additional uncertainty and dispersion across industries.

We think it makes sense to underweight cyclical industries, CCC-rated corporates - which account for the bulk of defaults - and lower-rated securitized debt, as these are most vulnerable. Mixing higher-yielding sectors across the rating spectrum - including high-yield corporates, emerging market debt and securitized assets - provides further diversification.

Temper equity volatility with high yield. Historically, high yield bonds have delivered returns comparable to equities but with meaningfully less volatility - and have generally outperformed equities in periods of below-trend growth. In our view, that makes high yield a credible complement for investors aiming to ease equity volatility without materially sacrificing return potential.

Harness a systematic approach. Today’s environment also increases potential alpha from security selection. Active systematic fixed-income approaches may help investors harvest these opportunities. Systematic strategies rely on a range of predictive factors that aren’t efficiently captured through traditional investing. Because systematic approaches depend on different performance drivers, we believe their returns complement traditional active strategies.

Protect against inflation. We think investors should consider increasing their allocations to inflation strategies, given the risk of future surges in inflation and inflation’s corrosive effect.

Staying Disciplined in a Disrupted Landscape

Taken together, we believe these elements create a more resilient fixed-income foundation. In our view, diversified sources of duration, balanced rate and credit exposures, and ample liquidity provide a framework that can absorb uncertainty while remaining nimble enough to quickly capture fresh opportunities as they arise.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: EWP: Spain Steadies Itself After A March Correction, Low-Teens P/E
data.description:
A year ago, President Trump trotted out those now-infamous posterboards detailing extremely high reciprocal tariff rates. So-called “Liberation Day" from the White House Rose Garden sparked a global stock market selloff. Today, there are similar vibes—just swap out “trade war” for “Iran war.” Equities are off the highs, bond yields are up, and volatility has spiked.

Another parallel is relative weakness in overseas stocks. Europe has been hit hard. Spain, namely, is well off its early-year record high. Today, I see value in the bank-heavy, value-focused country ETF. I have a "Buy" rating on the iShares MSCI Spain ETF (EWP). I’ll provide color on the fundamentals, valuation, and technicals. Seasonality also looks strong as we head into the middle part of the year.

Spain Outperforming YoY

Europe P/E Dips to 14x

According to the issuer, EWP seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of Spanish equities. It provides exposure to large and mid-sized companies in Spain and is used to express a single country view.

EWP is a small ETF, with just $1.8 billion in assets under management as of April 1, 2026. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 50 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about a full percentage point above that of the S&P 500 at 2.23%.

Share-price momentum is rated pristinely by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system—an A+. I’ll point out later, however, that the technicals and absolute price strength have deteriorated significantly just in the past five weeks. And while EWP Risk Grade looks good, the portfolio is highly concentrated, with two sectors dominating the allocation.

Finally, on liquidity, EWP has mixed metrics. Average daily volume is north of 800,000 shares over the past 30 days, while the 30-day median bid/ask spread is moderate at 9 basis points. That’s high enough for me to encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market open.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 2-star ETF by Morningstar plots along the very top row of the style box, indicating its high allocation to Spanish large caps. Just 9% of the fund is classified as growth, with a notable 22% in value. Large-cap blend is far and away the largest weighting.

In terms of the valuation, EWP’s price-to-earnings ratio is very low at just 13.2x as of the end of February. After a March drubbing, that multiple is likely even lower to kick off the second quarter. Pair the low-teens P/E with a long-term EPS growth rate of 10.2%, and the resulting PEG ratio is very compelling at below 1.4x.

EWP: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, Financials commands a high 42% position. Utilities is another notable overweight compared to the power-generation sector’s S&P 500 position. Those two value-oriented areas make up almost two-thirds of EWP.

Unfortunately, Energy is less than 5%, so it has not helped the ETF (France (EWQ) has a higher Energy stake in Europe). On the growth end of the style spectrum, Technology is a mere 5.4% of the Spanish equity ETF. So, investors should expect alpha if we see global Information Technology stocks underperform.

EWP: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, EWP has tended to perform very well in April and May over the past 10 years of price data. This augurs for a rebound following a steep 5.1% March 2026 plunge.

EWP: Bullish April-May Trends

The Technical Take

With an attractive valuation and bullish calendar trends, EWP’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares plummeted 12% from the February high to the March low. A strong bounce just recently only brings the Spain ETF back to its flat 50-day moving average—there's still major work for the bulls to do.

The good news is that the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. The 200dma is also below the price, which is a positive.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It printed a series of lower highs at the tail end of the 2025-2026 rally to record highs. That was bearish divergence, portending the late-Q1 decline. Today, EWP remains in a high-congestion zone (evidenced by the volume by price profile on the left side of the chart).

The takeaway? We may see EWP trade sideways for a while. A correction through price occurred in March, another one through time could unfold into mid-year. Support is near $49.

EWP: $49 Support, Rising 200dma, Uptrend Broken

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on EWP. Shares have corrected, but the technical situation is not an all-clear. But the valuation is compelling, particularly if we see a rebound in banks and Utilities shares.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Anthropic Tries To Contain Claude Code Instruction Leak
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Anthropic scrambles after Claude Code instructions leak exposes internal tools. (0:15) Tesla deliveries miss expectations. (1:04) Hiring plans jump sharply in March despite slower 2026 outlook. (1:55)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Out top story so far, Anthropic (ANTHRO) is working to contain fallout after accidentally revealing internal instructions used to direct its AI coding agent, Claude Code.

The Wall Street Journal reports that Anthropic initially issued a broad copyright takedown request to remove more than 8,000 copies of the raw instructions shared on GitHub, before narrowing the request to 96 posts after saying the first sweep reached more accounts than intended.

A spokesperson said the leak of “some internal source code” did not expose customer data or the core model weights — the mathematical parameters that power its AI models. However, it did reveal commercially sensitive techniques and tools used to enable Claude Code to function as a coding agent.

Those tools, known as a harness, allow users to control and direct the model. The leak effectively gives rivals and developers insight into how to replicate key features without reverse engineering.

Among active stocks, Tesla (TSLA) delivered about 358K vehicles in Q1, missing the 365K consensus estimate. Production totaled more than 408K vehicles. Model 3/Y deliveries came in near 342K, with other models accounting for roughly 16K.

Shares of Bed Bath & Beyond (BBBY) are rebounding after the company announced plans to acquire several assets, including The Container Store, which emerged from bankruptcy last year and is now creditor-owned.

Steel and aluminum names including Nucor (NUE), Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF), ArcelorMittal (MT), and Alcoa (AA) are under pressure after The Wall Street Journal reported that finished products containing imported steel and aluminum would face a 25% tariff.

The tariff would apply to the entire value of derivative products containing those metals, replacing the current structure that applies duties only to the value of the steel or aluminum component.

Looking to the economy, with the official March jobs report arriving on Good Friday — a market but not federal holiday — Challenger’s report showed an uptick in hiring plans.

Employers announced 32,826 planned hires in March, up 157% from February and 149% from a year earlier.

Still, announced hiring plans for 2026 total 50,887 through March, down 6% from the same period in 2025.

In other news of note, McDonald’s (MCD) is expanding its value offerings as it looks to win back budget-conscious consumers.

Beginning in April, the chain plans to roll out menu items priced at $3 or less, alongside a $4 breakfast deal and lunch and dinner meal bundles starting at $5.

Under-$3 breakfast options include the Sausage McMuffin, Sausage Biscuit, Sausage Burrito, Hash Browns, and medium McCafé Premium Roast Coffee. Lunch and dinner items at that price point include the McChicken, McDouble, 4-Piece McNuggets, small fries, and a medium soft drink.

The company is also bringing its Meal Deal back to breakfast.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BofA’s equity team is flagging stocks that hedge funds are heavily shorting, suggesting they could be candidates for a short squeeze on positive fundamental news.

Those names include Moderna (MRNA), Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Best Buy (BBY), Skyworks Solutions (SWKS), and Coinbase (COIN).

Check out the full list here.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Quiet Turnaround In UnitedHealth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) is flat since my last coverage, but the underlying business is significantly improved. The past two months have seen the management articulate their AI strategy, committing $1.5 billion to save $1 billion in costs. They’ve accelerated their automation in claims, pharmacy, and revenue cycles.

This is not just efficiency but margin expansion. The operating cost ratio is targeting 12.8%, with AI processing millions of interactions daily at high resolution rates. Meanwhile, the integration of the Optum platform is resulting in increased client stickiness. The market is not seeing it. I believe the business is compounding efficiency and is ready for the next leg up.

$1.5 Billion AI Allocation Extracts $1 Billion to hit a 12.8% Operating Cost Ratio in 2026

In my view, AI integration and overhead cost extraction form an operating advantage for UnitedHealth as it allocates $1.5 billion toward AI platforms in 2026 to extract $1 billion in operating costs. Optum Insight and Optum Financial Services merge to process healthcare transactions. Optum Real executes point-of-care approvals and monetizes claims instantly, and this shift removes post-service reconciliation. Providers receive cash faster.

As a result, UnitedHealth decreases admin labor, and operating cost ratios decrease due to this automation. UnitedHealth targets a 12.8% operating cost ratio for 2026, as the 2025 operating cost ratio is at 13.3%. AI answers 3 million calls per day, and the system hits an 18-second answer speed and resolves inquiries at a 90% rate on the first contact. With that, advocates redirect focus to escalated cases.

Additionally, OptumRx removes reauthorization mandates for 180 drugs that is reducing prior authorizations by 10%, and this automation removes friction in the pharmacy supply chain. OptumRx implements cost-based reimbursement models and passes 100% of rebates to clients that helped win 800 client contracts, and it expands operating margins by 0.2 percentage points in 2026.

Moreover, I see Optum Health consolidating electronic medical records. With that, employed provider groups operate on one of three platforms, which is down from 18 platforms. This consolidation feeds data into AI models uniformly. As a result, value-based care cohorts hit medical costs 30% below market averages, and patient satisfaction scores reach 90. Optum Health margin targets forecast a 2026 expansion of 0.3 percentage points, and Optum Insight margin targets forecast an expansion of 0.9 percentage points.

Further, revenue cycle management software utilizes AI to decrease human labor. For instance, Crimson AI saves money for hospital clients and increases software sales for UnitedHealth, and the automation extracts overhead and accelerates cash conversion. UnitedHealth derived $19.7 billion in cash from operations in 2025, with the 2026 cash flow target of $18 billion. These cash flow funds dividends, share repurchases, and acquisitions amid ROCE at 12.91%.

I believe decreasing operating costs counteracts medical care ratio (MCR) increases. UnitedHealth expects an MCR of 88.8% for 2026, with automating functions protects the net income margin. The net income margin currently hit 2.69%, with operating margins at 4.24%. EBITDA margins are at 4.83%. UnitedHealth projects $17.75 in adjusted EPS for 2026 under topline projections of $439 billion. Notably, UnitedHealth already recorded 2025 revenues of $448 billion, but with 2026 revenue, UnitedHealthcare targets margin expansion of 0.4 percentage points.

With the insurance unit implementing AI to process claims and manage enrollment, UnitedHealth reduces workforce headcount and rationalizes real estate assets to lower overhead. The $2.5 billion restructuring charge in 2025 included severance and lease terminations, and with that, UnitedHealth exits loss-making contracts. Optum Health drops non-aligned preferred provider organization contracts, and risk membership drops by 15%, which removes ancillary service risk.

I see UnitedHealth scales its operations without adding personnel by removing human labor from admin tasks. AI systems learn/adapt from daily interactions and compound cost savings over time, and the $1.5 billion investment in 2026 supports product development. Optum Financial settles payments instantly and replaces legacy billing processes, so this software ecosystem binds payers and providers to UnitedHealth platforms. UnitedHealth sells revenue cycle products to outside hospitals. Here, 8 out of 10 hospitals utilize UnitedHealth software, and 8 out of 10 payers utilize UnitedHealth software. Therefore, AI upgrades increase the value of existing software contracts.

Further, UnitedHealth manages $83 billion in total debt, and it targets a 40% debt-to-capital ratio by 2026’s end. Stable cash flow from automated operations pays down this debt. As a result, regular maturities occurring in H1-2026 receive cash payments. UnitedHealth has capital for stock repurchases as capital deployment may remain active due to overhead cost reductions.

As dividend yield reaches 3.41%, it is supported by cash per share of $26.89. UnitedHealth offsets membership losses through admin savings, and it is lowering the cost to serve patients and providers that protects the income statement from funding cuts. An asset turnover ratio of 1.47x means to me that utilization of assets to derive sales. For 2026, OptumRx forecasts earnings growth expanding into the high single-digit to low double-digit range, and Optum Insight projects earnings growth above 4%. With that, OptumHealth targets operating earnings growth near 9%, while UnitedHealthcare targets operating earnings growth near 13%.

Lastly, the integration of Optum Real and Optum Financial Services unlocks value pools for payers. So, UnitedHealth intends to automate 70% to 90% of certain admin functions from start to finish, and this mass automation secures profitability amid the reimbursement environment.

Margin Squeeze vs. Machine Efficiency: Can AI Outrun Policy Headwinds?

The main risk I see for UNH is the widening gap between medical cost inflation and government reimbursement. In 2026, medical costs are expected to increase by ~10%, while the 2027 Medicare Advantage rate update results in a 0.09% increase. This has already resulted in a medical care ratio of 89.1% for 2025, while Q4 reached 92.4%.

Management has targeted 88.8% for 2026, but this is based on benefit cuts and footprint changes, which are already resulting in member losses. Medicare Advantage is expected to lose 1.3 to 1.4 million members as a result of these changes, while overall membership is expected to decline by as many as 2.8 million.

This is a result of prioritizing margins over growth but creates opportunities for competitors to gain market share. This is important, as two-thirds of revenue comes from government programs. In addition, OptumRx's rebate pass-through model hurts margins, while increases in Medicaid rates are still lagging cost inflation. ACA enrollment is also decreasing while margins are still only 1% as a result.

Lastly, regarding the valuation, UNH trades at ~15x 2026 EPS of $17.86 and ~13.5x 2027 EPS of $20.03, reflecting a slight discount to its historical premium multiple. On a revenue basis, the stock trades at ~0.5x forward sales, despite generating more than $440 billion in revenue annually, which again reflects the market's skepticism on margin sustainability.

The answer lies in the fact that while growth metrics look poor in the near term, that’s where the upside lies. As cost efficiencies start to take hold via the power of AI, even a modest re-rating to historical multiples will provide a nice upside. I think we’re witnessing a classic case of sentiment discounting execution. If UnitedHealth Group meets its efficiency targets, I believe the stock’s valuation represents a buying opportunity.

Takeaway

While UNH is not yet re-rating, the business is quietly getting better. AI-powered cost extraction is redefining margin improvement, not just cost savings. As automation continues to grow and Optum builds out its ecosystem, the quality of the earnings is getting better. Investors are worried about pressure, but they are missing the compounding model that can drive the next re-rating.

data.title: Wolters Kluwer: The Good And The Bad
data.description:
I'm maintaining a "Hold" rating for Wolters Kluwer N.V. (WOLTF) (WTKWY) (WKL.AS).

I have a favorable view of its portfolio restructuring initiatives. The company's sales, underpinned by extended-term agreements, are also protected in the short term.

But its reduced shareholder returns were disappointing. I worry about the threat from a credible upstart in the healthcare space too.

The previous November 6, 2025, write-up detailed WOLTF's solid 9M2025 topline metrics and the disruptive entry of "OpenEvidence/OE."

Competitor Is Gaining Traction

In early March, Wiley (WLY) revealed the integration of its "trusted scientific and medical content" including 400+ "journals and books" into OE.

The Value Proposition Of WOLTF's 'UpToDate/UTD'

I fear that the gap between OE and the other healthcare data players is gradually narrowing.

Another rival, Elsevier's ClinicalKey, draws on information from more than 600 academic titles. OpenEvidence is catching up in terms of content depth thanks to the WLY deal.

At its "Investor Teach-In" presentation (S&P Capital IQ transcript) on Dec 8, 2025, WOLTF highlighted UTD's focus on tracking "thousands of primary sources." This suggests to me that the access to a diverse range of peer-reviewed literature is a differentiating factor.

Reuters reported in late January that OE "raised $250 million in fresh funding." In my opinion, that could help to fund future alliances with other journal providers such as Informa's (IFPJF) Taylor & Francis.

My Nov '25 piece predicted that "WOLTF will find it tough to raise UTD's rates upon contract renewal" given OpenEvidence's "ad-supported model." This risk is materializing, considering the latter's growth momentum.

Cash Distribution Plan Was A Letdown

The stock's forward P/E has de-rated from a 52-week peak of 29.4x recorded in mid-2025 to 11.6x now. Its "Free Cash Flow/FCF" grew 10% to a substantial EUR1.35B last year.

You would have anticipated an increase in WOLTF's repurchases to take advantage of the cheaper shares. It has done the reverse instead.

The firm guided for a "2026 share buyback of up to €500 million" in its FY25 results release issued in February.

WOLTF has spent at least EUR1.0 billion every year to purchase its own stock in FY2022-2026. It traded at a more demanding 25-30 times earnings multiple range during this period. I don't think it makes sense to buy fewer shares when they get cheaper.

Also, the lower-than-expected share count reduction could hurt its bottom-line accretion. According to S&P Capital IQ, WOLTF's EPS expansion is projected to moderate from FY25's +6.4% to FY26's +5.9%.

But Portfolio Reshaping Moves Are Value-Accretive

I like how WOLTF is evolving its business mix through acquisitions and disposals.

The company disclosed the divestiture of its "Finance, Risk & Regulatory Reporting" or "FRR" unit in the 4Q25 slides. I believe this is the right action to take for a non-core and low-margin business.

FRR accounted for less than one-tenth of WOLTF's FY2025 turnover. This division boasted "mid-single-digit (EBIT) margins" based on the quarterly briefing remarks. The group's "Operating Profit Margin/OPM" was a much higher 28% last year. I estimate that its FY26 company-wide OPM can receive a 0.5ppts boost from FRR's separation.

Separately, WOLTF allocated around EUR900M to M&A in the prior year. Two of the acquirees that caught my eye are "Registered Agent Solutions Inc/RASi" and "BrightFlag/BF."

At the Q4 call, the firm drew attention to its core "enterprise customer base." You can expect the diversification into new user groups to offer a new growth lever. It indicated in the earnings presentation that BF and RASi are primarily targeting the "mid-size corporate segment."

Near-Term Defensiveness Is Also Underrated

My take is that WOLTF's financials should remain fairly resilient for the foreseeable future despite OpenEvidence's challenge.

Roughly 83% of the group's FY25 top line was recurrent in nature. UTD's churn rate was lower than 10% in the same timeframe.

You would think that clients are pretty happy with the UpToDate tool. WOLTF shared at the December 8, 2025, investor meeting that "it consistently had a Net Promoter Score above 70."

It's taking steps to further enhance revenue predictability as well. The company mentioned at the 4Q2025 meeting that "we signed more multiyear contracts for longer durations with higher annual contract value" in '25 versus preceding years.

UpToDate's AI Agent Add-On

Also, more than 30% of WOLTF's healthcare customers have adopted UTD's latest AI-powered feature called "Expert AI" outlined above. In my view, this may slow down the potential migration of users to the AI-native OE.

Valuation Analysis

I see the stock as being reasonably priced.

Peer Benchmarking

Its EBITDA multiple is at a slight discount or below 0.3 turns below the listed comparable average.

My Concluding Thoughts

I've stayed "Neutral" on this name.

WOLTF is fairly valued. There are both positive and negative read-throughs from recent events.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Major Market Rotation Is Likely Coming: Here's Where I'm Loading Up
data.description:
The first quarter of 2026 was one of the most headline-whiplash quarters investors have experienced in years, with numerous forces driving market rotations, including:

  • SaaS-pocalypse fears

  • Mega-cap tech CapEx announcements for AI

  • Private credit concerns

  • Uncertainty about where inflation and interest rates are headed

  • And, most recently, the Iran war

These factors have all aligned to set the market up for significant rotation, with three sectors in particular positioned to rebound sharply. In this article, I will take a deeper look at that macro environment for the past three months, as well as why I think a major market shift may be ahead.

AI Euphoria Meets a Rude Awakening

The S&P 500 (SPY) opened the year hovering near all-time highs and indeed hit an all-time high in January, due to strong AI CapEx announcements, such as Amazon's (AMZN) $200 billion, Meta's (META) $115 billion to $135 billion, and similarly strong announcements from the likes of Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Oracle (ORCL). Additionally, consumer confidence and retail sales data seemed healthy heading into February, leading to subdued volatility.

However, as the calendar turned from January to February, the first major shock hit as reports of advanced AI tools potentially disrupting the cybersecurity and software spaces fed what was broadly termed as the "SaaS-pocalypse." This is the fear that AI would not only challenge but ultimately disrupt and even displace significant portions of the software industry, causing the sector to plunge, with even sector stalwarts like MSFT, Adobe (ADBE), and CrowdStrike (CRWD) getting hit hard, while more broadly diversified software names like Roper Technologies (ROP) also did not escape. Even AI darlings like Palantir (PLTR) were not immune to these headwinds, and software lenders like Hercules Capital (HTGC) got hit hard over concerns that software loans themselves might even be impaired, beyond just software equity (IGV).

War, Oil, and the Inflation Specter

Meanwhile, the Iran war in late February and into March provided another major shock to the markets, as it sent oil soaring above $100 a barrel. Given that about 20% of global oil supply transits through the Strait of Hormuz and was disrupted by the conflict, this very important supply chain disruption pushed energy prices (XLE) higher. It also drove fears that inflation may re-accelerate, as rising energy costs and restricted global supply chains have second- and third-order effects that can push up the prices of virtually every good and service in the economy, while also raising the risk of potential escalating tensions and even open conflict between major powers like the U.S. and China or the U.S. and Russia, given that Iran is a strategic partner of both of those countries.

This weighed heavily on the broader markets, sending them lower, while the energy sector soared higher. Particularly interest-rate-sensitive names staggered under the soaring energy prices, as it reduced the chances that the Fed would cut rates and that long-term interest rates would fall as well. As a result, names like Realty Income (O) dipped sharply in the wake of the Iran war, despite being broadly viewed as a bond proxy safe haven during periods of economic weakness.

Another concern is that the U.S. continues to run a huge budget deficit, and this budget deficit will likely only be exacerbated further by this point in time, as the Pentagon is expected to request an additional $200 billion to fund the war and restock weapon supplies that are being rapidly depleted in the conflict.

While Q1 was not a pleasant one for the broader markets, fortunately, my portfolio was well diversified into energy and midstream stocks, so I am up significantly year to date and am significantly outperforming the S&P 500 so far. Looking ahead, I see three sectors that are positioned to rebound, and am opportunistically rotating capital into them in anticipation of this recovery.

Beaten-Down Industrials Hiding a Massive Rebound Setup

The first one I'm going to discuss is industrials (XLI), and particularly those that are sensitive to high energy prices. Yes, the higher energy prices will likely weigh on these companies in the near term, and higher energy prices could persist for longer. However, I believe over the long term, energy prices will normalize, and when they do, these industrial stocks, especially high-quality ones with solid balance sheets, will rebound very sharply.

For example, United Parcel Service (UPS) has gotten hit due to increased diesel and jet fuel costs, as well as having to reroute flights around the Middle East. Meanwhile, Amcor (AMCR) is another business that is engaged in packaging production and therefore has seen its cost of plastics, which are energy-price sensitive, soar. This, in turn, has pushed both companies' dividend yields above 7%, or close to 7%, on a forward-looking basis, despite having strong investment-grade credit ratings, competitively positioned business models, and the ability to recover some of these charges through fuel surcharges for UPS and cost pass-through contract clauses for AMCR.

Though both will likely see some negative impacts, I think the sell-off in both of these has been overblown, along with several other industrials, creating strong recovery potential if the market does undergo a rotation as the Iran war is expected to wind down in the next few weeks.

Gold and Miners: The Dip That Looks Like A Huge Gift

Another sector that I believe is positioned to rebound strongly is precious metals and miners (GDX), which got hit hard in the wake of the Iran war, largely due to a combination of expectations of higher-for-longer interest rates, which are generally a negative for gold, as well as the market sell-off, which is likely causing some heavily invested, heavily leveraged traders to sell their gold positions to meet margin calls. Additionally, the dollar strengthened in the wake of global geopolitical uncertainty, which in turn weighed on gold in dollar terms.

However, these headwinds are short-term in nature, and I believe the Iran war actually strengthens gold's long-term bull case, as war spending equals more debt issuance, which in turn puts pressure on the dollar over the long term and, in turn, is very bullish for gold. Additionally, geopolitical macroeconomic uncertainty caused by energy price volatility and global conflicts is very bullish for gold over time, while this war will likely only further accelerate de-dollarization, given the strain it's placing on U.S. alliances, especially in Europe.

Thus, I think that the dip in gold and gold miners is an incredible gift for investors, and that they can take advantage of it by loading up on blue-chip, undervalued gold miners, as well as gold ETFs (GLD), silver ETFs (SLV), and gold and silver bullion leases, which are actually my favorite way to invest in precious metals, given that you can generate a very attractive yield along with relatively low-risk full ownership of bullion.

Why the Private Credit Panic Could Be the Best Buying Opportunity of 2026

The third and final sector I expect to benefit from a market rotation in the coming months is the private credit space (PBDC). This is because private credit has gotten hit by both the SaaS-pocalypse fears, since many BDCs and private credit funds have significant exposure to software loans. The broader BDC and private credit spaces have largely been out of favor, with major media attacks and some retail funds experiencing elevated withdrawal requests.

However, I think the fears are way overblown compared to the realities in the sector, as BDCs have fairly modest leverage levels and plenty of liquidity, along with very strong underwriting quality, with non-accruals well within historical norms. Their counterparties continue to grow EBITDA and revenue at a strong clip.

Additionally, the SaaS-pocalypse fears, I think, are overblown, given that most software loans are at 30% or even lower on a loan-to-value basis, while the sector itself has delivered very resilient returns over time, even when the environment for the S&P 500 was experiencing poor performance. When looking at leading BDCs (BIZD) like Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), Main Street Capital (MAIN), Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), and several others, I'm seeing sustained stability in their underwriting performance and view the massive pullback as a huge opportunity for investors to take advantage of deeply discounted companies with well-diversified and well-performing investments.

Additionally, some of the alternative asset managers are also very attractive, with leading names like Blackstone (BX) and Ares Management (ARES) posting strong assets under management growth and even significant dividend growth. While the private credit headlines will likely take a bite out of their growth momentum in the near term, I think these businesses are well diversified even beyond private credit and over time should continue to grow at a solid pace, which combines with their attractive dividend yields to deliver attractive risk-reward potential for investors who are patient and are willing to buy now while it is out of favor.

Why I'm Loading Up Now, And What I'm Buying

While many investors dread quarters like the one we recently had, with the S&P 500 down, tech getting hit hard, and disruption, uncertainty, and volatility abundant, this is actually my dream investing environment. Not only have I been hedged against this sort of outcome for quite some time, given my overweight exposure to energy, but I also view such periods of volatility as creating major disconnects in prices relative to intrinsic value. This gives me the opportunity to actively manage my portfolio and opportunistically recycle capital.

As a result, at High Yield Investor, I am currently loading up on attractive opportunities in:

  • Precious metals

  • Private credit

  • Alternative asset managers

  • Industrials

  • And several other undervalued opportunities

…in anticipation of a strong recovery for these sectors in the months to come. I expect this will help to continue fueling my significant total return outperformance with below-market beta, all while generating a 7–8% dividend yield in my portfolio.

data.title: Energy Transfer: A Safer Bet In Today's Chaos
data.description:
ET Looks Like A Safe-Haven Yield Play

I don't think I shall retell all the news of the past few weeks to make sure you understand what I mean by the "chaos" in the title of this article. As I'm writing these lines, both the S&P 500 (SPX) and the NASDAQ Composite (US100:IND) futures are falling by over 1% while the crude oil futures rise again (this time by 7.60%) following Trump's threats to hit Iran "extremely hard".

In this environment, I was thinking about potentially safe assets that can weather the storm if consumer inflation ticks up again, making its second wave after COVID. Last time we saw abnormally high inflation rates - the 2021-2023 cycle - a lot of MLPs like Energy Transfer (ET) felt great. And even when the inflation subsided, ET, continuing this example, kept rising higher:

In today's environment, it looks obvious that when crude oil prices jump, energy names tend to follow, and when the markets calm down, the pumped-up stock/unit prices follow as well. But as we can see above, it's not entirely true. I believe ET can repeat its outstanding performance, having limited valuation contraction during the next (possible) inflation overheating. The massive long-term contracts to supply natural gas to AI-driven data centers that Energy Transfer has already secured are not going anywhere, which is making this pick not just a safe haven, but also a GARP pick among MLPs.

My Updated Reasoning On ET Units

My previous research article on ET was basically a preview piece, as I posted in late January. Since then, ET has already reported for its fiscal Q4 2025, showing an adjusted EBITDA of ~$4.18 billion (+8% YoY) at $25.32 billion revenue. The analysts were expecting $24.38 billion in Q4 top-line (+29.5% YoY), so ET has beaten the consensus by over 5%, according to Seeking Alpha. The partnership's results were largely driven by higher volumes in key divisions like NGL fractionation, LPG exports, and crude oil transportation. The EPS came in at a lower-than-expected level again, but for MLPs, and ET in particular, analysts usually fail to properly predict the bottom line. What matters more here is the DCF (distributable cash flows), which was quite strong, coming in at ~$2.04 billion (last year, we saw $1.98 billion in Q4, so the latest figure looks at least consistent).

I didn't mention it last time, but ET's affiliate, USA Compression Partners, successfully closed its acquisition of J-W Power Company in late January 2026, helping ET's management upgrade its FY2026 EBITDA guidance because ET is going to add a lot of high-pressure compression capacity to its consolidated footprint in the coming months. With that addition, they can satisfy more NG demand in the Permian and Haynesville basins.

We now expect our 2026 adjusted EBITDA to range between $17.45 billion and $17.85 billion compared to the previous range of between $17.3 billion and $17.7 billion. This change in guidance is solely attributable to the USA Compression's acquisition of J-W Power Company, which closed on January 12, 2026. Looking ahead, we are poised for continued growth in 2026, driven largely by the ramp of our Flexport NGL export project, new Permian processing plants and other projects.

Source: ET's Q4 earnings call

After suspending the ET's Lake Charles LNG in December 2025, Energy Transfer remains a pipeline operator at its core, and we see from the above growth CAPEX plans that they're going to self-fund a lot of domestic pipelines, which I view as highly accretive in the long term. ET upsized its planned Desert Southwest Pipeline expansion from a 42-inch to a 48-inch diameter, and it's going to be completed by 2029. Once done, the massive 520-mile system will be carrying ~2.3 Bcf/d of natural gas from the Permian Basin to Arizona and the Southwest, where plenty of data centers are located. The demand on that end market is as strong as I previously expected, which is why ET has already signed for natural gas deliveries to an Oracle data center campus in Texas. Starting in 2028, Entergy Louisiana will start to transport 250,000 MMBtu/d, and based on a separate agreement with CloudBurst, they'll supply up to 450,000 MMBtu/d.

I think we need to figure out the size and the net impact of the above contracts and all the new demand that most people didn't even know could have existed for ET 5-7 years ago. Within the last year alone, Energy Transfer has contracted 6 Bcf/d of pipeline capacity specifically to "demand-pull" customers (we include DCs, utilities, and all end-users here). These contracts have a weighted average life of 18 years, and when taken together, they should generate over $25 billion in firm transportation fees - it was the estimate back from November 2025. I believe in 2026, there will be more, but for what's already contracted, ET has basically locked in ~10% of total daily capacity through long-term agreements in just the past year. The Desert Southwest Pipeline's upsizing boost before the construction even began indirectly hints at possibly more demand ahead, and likely at higher pricing. Projects like this are going to bring in massive ROIC, and the level of predictability here is much higher compared to the ROIC levels hyperscalers would have from their current CAPEX, in my opinion.

Given our extensive backlog of potential growth projects, we continue to be extremely focused on capital discipline, and we'll continue to target projects that are expected to generate the highest returns while balancing project risk. We continue to target a long-term annual distribution growth rate of 3% to 5%. We also expect to maintain our leverage target of 4x to 4.5x EBITDA during this period of meaningful investment opportunities.

Source: ET's Q4 earnings call

The end energy demand is strong, and the current projects that require growth CAPEX look promising. What I like about ET as well is its inflation protection - this is the reason I laid out initially in this article.

If we think about it, with higher inflation, there come higher interest rates, hence higher required rates of investors' returns. ET's business is a massive cash generation machine that operates on a 90% fee-based toll-road model, meaning that they're paid based on the physical volume of hydrocarbons moving through their pipelines, not the underlying price of those commodities. The potential pitfall here lies in demand disruption - it was the case in the past, when inflation rises, costs follow, and so households/factories cut back on energy use. Now, the firm's AI focus has changed the game - ET is pivoting aggressively toward "demand-pull" customers, the way I discussed above. And their new contracts are quite long-term (as I noted, 18 years on average), so the partnership's FCF generation capacity starts to look more protected than it used to. Also very important - Energy Transfer's distribution yield looks reasonable for safe-haven seekers amid the higher odds of the Fed's pause (or even a new interest rate rising cycle ahead).

With better financial leverage and better contract structure, I believe ET should have a distribution yield of 7% on the forward basis. Now it's 7.46%, so there should be repricing to the upside of ~6.5% from today's unit price:

Consensus FY2027 payout / Yield = Fair price per unit

$1.42 / 0.07 = $20.29 (6.5% upside from today's $19/unit)

Source: Oakoff's calculations

Adding the yield itself to the above nominal price upside potential, I get an undervaluation of 13-14%, with limited risks to possible inflation upticks in the next quarters.

Risks

As I noted in my January update on ET, the firm is now actively pivoting its corporate strategy quite noticeably, and this can result in a dramatic need for new growth CAPEX that I couldn't foresee or detect. It creates certain execution risks around updated growth CAPEX plans and deleveraging. In addition to that, the spikes in oil prices that we see might indeed cause some demand destruction, at least in non-AI areas. Plus, possibly higher inflation drives up all costs, so ET's OPEX side will be inflated as well (labor, electricity, materials, etc.), and CAPEX plans might need further adjustments (to the negative side). Finally, the undervaluation potential that I've described above may be too modest even for yield-seeking investors. There might be other high-yield MLPs on the market, so the opportunity cost is something to always keep in mind.

Your Takeaway

While I agree that some risks look quite scary to those investors looking for an energy-related safe haven today, I believe Energy Transfer units should provide some stability in case you're expecting an inflation comeback shortly. I keep my "Buy" rating unchanged for now, seeing a total return potential of 13-14% in the next 12 months.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: TD Synnex: I See More Upside As Hyve Continues To Deliver
data.description:
Thesis

TD SYNNEX Corporation (SNX) is basically a middleman for the tech world, but a really important one. Companies that build the digital backbone (laptops, servers, software, cloud systems) don’t typically deal directly with every business or corner shop. That’s a needlessly sloppy and inefficient way to go about it. Instead, they rely on companies like SNX to handle that whole process. It purchases large quantities of technology products and services, then sells them to many smaller businesses, such as IT consultants, resellers, and managed service providers. Those smaller businesses then sell, set up, or support these products and services for the final customers. It’s not complicated once you see it. They basically run on margin plus services.

It's been a while since I last covered SNX with a Buy based on its undervaluation, steady earnings growth, and exposure to higher-growth areas like cloud and data infrastructure. Since then, shares have delivered an exceptional total return, comfortably outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) by a wide margin.

Fresh off Q1 2026 earnings and a predictably upbeat market response, the stock is back on its upward track. And after going over the latest data, I’m still leaning bullish, as the core business remains resilient and the growth drivers behind its recent momentum are still firmly in place.

Breakdown of TD SYNNEX’s Q1 2026 Earnings

  • Distribution gross billings growth: +17% YoY
  • Hyve gross billings growth: +95% YoY.

My first read is that the quarter’s strength came from two very different parts of the business both doing well simultaneously. Looking at the distribution side of things, company’s management said demand was strong across multiple areas: infrastructure, security, software, PCs, and even networking, which had been weak before but is now starting to recover. In Hyve, I wasn't surprised to see that the main reason for growth was strong demand from hyperscaler customers, it's all about the ongoing AI buildout. And most of this growth's still coming from the biggest customers, although new projects are expected to start picking up later in the year. It helps explain why revenue, margins, and profitability all rose together.

  • Revenue: $17.16B (+18% YoY)
  • Non-GAAP EPS: $4.73 (+69% YoY)
  • Non-GAAP operating income growth: +48% YoY.

On the distribution side, the main issue was pricing. Management says rising memory and component costs helped justify higher average selling prices. This also caused some customers to buy earlier than planned. However, they emphasized that these effects were noticeable but not very large or extreme.

  • ASP / pull-forward impact: ~2 pts of growth.

I think that makes sense. The latest industry data does back up their point. For example, TrendForce said this week that contract prices for DRAM and NAND memory are rising quite quickly. This is because more of the available supply is being used for servers and enterprise SSDs, leaving less supply for the rest of the market and making overall availability tighter. IDC has also said that the memory shortage is now increasing total PC revenues, even though fewer units are being sold. This matches what management is saying, that higher ASPs (average selling prices) can make up for some drop in demand, especially in commercial PCs more than in consumer PCs.

  • Distribution operating margin: 2.0% (+34 bps YoY).

That helps explain why their profit margins actually increased. More or less, what the management team's really saying is that they made smart decisions. They bought their products at the right times, stayed in close and careful contact with both their suppliers and their customers, and used their inventory prudently so they could absorb price changes instead of scrambling after the fact.

I think that makes sense in the short term. But I wouldn’t get carried away and assume this is the new normal. Larger distributors, with strong supplier connections and buying power, can take advantage of supply disruptions better than smaller competitors. Even so, this doesn’t necessarily mean their higher profits will last long-term.

Meanwhile, customers were doing a bit better than expected. That said, there were still some signs things could be unstable. Orders waiting to be filled are increasing, there are lots of price quotes being requested, and customers are placing orders earlier than usual because prices don’t stay valid for long and some products are still hard to get. So this tends to give the company a clearer short-term view of demand, but it doesn’t remove the risk. Customers may just be picking their spots more carefully on timing and purchases and management did flag that demand could fall off in the second half.

  • Hyve operating income growth: +66% YoY
  • Hyve operating margin: 4.2%.

Hyve's definitely the more interesting piece strategically. Its revenue mix and margins are shifting with AI infrastructure. But management was fairly clear that the recent spike is still mostly coming from just two big customers. And you can’t ignore that concentration risk. At the same time, they are landing new hyperscale programs and moving into more complete rack- and system-level builds. I think that should make them more relevant to customers and, over time, probably help their positioning.

On balance, the broader setup does seem to support it. In mid-March, DIGITIMES reported that hyperscalers are increasing their spending, which is boosting demand across the entire AI server supply chain. This includes parts like interconnects and circuit boards. In other words, it’s not just them, it’s a wider trend hitting the whole industry.

  • Free cash flow (Q1): $(929)M
  • Cash: $1.6B
  • Leverage: 1.5x.

Cash flow was the only part that didn’t look as strong as the income statement at first glance, but there’s a clear reason for it. The company used up cash in its day-to-day operations because it deliberately increased inventory in its distribution network and kept investing money to support Hyve’s growth. A clear positive is the strong cash position and relatively reasonable debt levels. And this puts them in a good position to keep investing in the business while also returning money to shareholders.

Above, you can see for yourselves SNX's superior dividend growth rate over the broader market (SPY).

Overall, Europe's still holding up well rather than getting worse. According to IDC, IT spending in Europe, the Middle East, and Africa is expected to grow this year, mainly driven by software. Businesses and service providers are still spending at a healthy level, even though consumer spending remains weak. Given that environment, management’s aiming to grow faster than the overall market in Europe, focusing on helping medium and large businesses upgrade and modernize their systems, and being careful with acquisitions, that is, targeting deals with solid returns instead of just getting bigger for the sake of it.

SNX’s Valuation and Risk Profile

I think it’s fair to say that SNX still appears a bit undervalued when you look at its earnings multiple, but not by a large amount. The stock's trading below the fair value range suggested by the FAST Graphs analysis, and its current valuation is also clearly lower than the typical levels seen across the sector, both based on past earnings and expected future earnings.

That lower valuation makes sense because this is a distribution business, which typically gets lower valuation multiples. It’s also more sensitive to economic cycles, has naturally thinner profit margins, and strong earnings tend to trigger questions about how long they’ll hold up.

That said, the outlook starts to raise some flags. Expected revenue growth of only 5.4% and long-term earnings per share growth of around 10% are not especially strong. Their healthy balance sheet and better management of working capital give them some cushion to handle challenges. But if growth slows down, the current lower valuation may end up being justified rather than a bargain.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I think SNX's still a good Buy. There’s room for gains if Hyve continues to grow steadily and keeps its profit margins in distribution stable. Meanwhile, balance sheet strength and valuation provide some downside support. It fits for investors seeking a reasonably priced compounder with AI infrastructure exposure who can accept cyclicality.

data.title: Simon Property Group: Implications On The Passing Of The CEO
data.description:
Simon Property Group (SPG) announced the passing of David Simon, CEO (Chairman Of The Board and President as well), who was really the single most important person in the company ever since it went public. Along with the announcement came a declaration that his son Eli Simon would become the new CEO with an independent non-executive chairman.

Rarely does any company replace someone like David Simon with another person at the same skill level. David Simon grew Simon property group into the investment grade company that it is today with probably one of the best diversification strategies in the business.

Because the primary advantage of a business (like the mall business that is the main concern here) is location, there is very unlikely to be a noticeable change at first. Instead, business will likely continue as usual for a while.

This is especially true as the company had long emphasized that there was a transition program in place just in case it was needed. So, there is every possibility that the son will be very capable to continue the business in adequate fashion.

All About Locations

The last article noted the latest dividend increase. Also noted in line with this was the conservative financial profile.

But now with the change in senior officers (and possibly a few more likely to be announced), what may make a difference in the future is location choices. There may also be a change in locations "that go downhill". These deteriorating situations need to be handled promptly, or it could at some point affect future growth.

But these things will be noticed over the years rather than a big immediate change from my last article. There is certainly no rush to make any investment decisions now.

Many of the decisions made in the past will likely last several years. That means the last article is unlikely to become obsolete any time soon.

The other thing to consider is that Simon Property Group is much larger now than it was when it first went public a few decades back. That means that future growth will be inevitably slower than it was just due to the size considerations.

So, what is considered a good job by top management has to be reframed to take into account the current company. Comparing the job performance of the new CEO to past accomplishments could well be an irrelevant comparison (and unfair as well). It is really about what superior management looks like going forward with a much larger company.

Stock Price

But if the current advantages are likely to last for a while, then the stock price performance should follow suit.

The stock price is up about one-third from its last high point until it reached $200 (give or take) recently. To me that means the growing dividend and the appreciation of the stock over time should probably continue to average about 10% per year.

Now if that slows materially, then it is time to evaluate the new management. Any growth story has a limit that is usually due to the skills of senior management. Simon, as one of the largest REIT's in the industry is likely to reach that upper limit sooner than many competitors.

However, this is a highly fractured industry. Therefore, there should be plenty of room for that slower growth mentioned earlier.

The current Quant System rating on the Seeking Alpha Website has the stock listed as a hold. Normally with the change in the CEO position, I would agree. However, the real estate business is largely a "location business". Either the company has a location advantage, or it does not. This company has that advantage.

Therefore, my best guess is that previous decisions have conferred an advantage that should show itself as continued industry outperformance for the next couple of years.

First Quarter

But that means that the first quarter should continue the long very good history that David Simon established before his passing. In fact, that very good record should continue for the rest of the fiscal year and probably the next year. To me that means a likely total return in the low teens for the foreseeable future unless something goes very wrong.

Now, there could be some inflation that may affect interest rates the company pays on its debt in the future. But the investment grade debt rating indicates that this company should be able to handle whatever challenges appear (financially).

The other consideration is that the malls themselves are likely to make up for inflation by charging higher rates. This might eventually overcome an initial interest rate boost (that might result in lower earnings initially). Inflation is generally seen as a long-term friend of real estate.

Summary

Rather than this be a hold as the quant system shows, I look at this as a long-term strong buy consideration. The timing as to when to get in may be currently uncertain due to the Iranian crisis. But to me, that is an execution decision as opposed to whether to rate this as a strong buy or not.

Simon Property Group has long been a leader in the REIT industry. I see that continuing for some time to come, despite the death of the CEO.

We will have to watch the new CEO establish an independent track record going forward. But that process takes place with the business currently having a big location and diversification advantage. Those advantages will not disappear "overnight".

Instead, there will be a gradual process of evaluating the long-term performance of the REIT as locations gradually change in value as time moves on. There should be a process set up whereby subpar locations are periodically eliminated (one way or another) while superior locations are added. For a company of the size of Simon, it will likely take time for these future decisions to become significant.

But individual investors and institutions can "grade" each decision as it is made so that an investment strategy can be formed as needed before there is too much backsliding (if that occurs).

No one is worried about the possibility that the new CEO is equally or more talented than the CEO that just passed away because that possibility does not involve a loss of principal.

Risks

The greatest risk appears to be a slowing economy combined with the initial effects of inflation. A slowing economy decreases the needs for malls (and shopping in general). Meanwhile the inflation threat increases the cost of debt as interest rates rise.

But in the long-run, no matter the situation, this company has outperformed the industry due to its diversification and location advantages. That outperformance is highly likely to continue.

Even if the stock were to drop as it did back in fiscal year 2020, the superior management strategy allowed the stock to recover all losses while a recent acquisition shows the growth strategy remains in place. Growing the business allows the market to see a pathway to a higher stock value in the future.

I personally tend to hold something like this until either stock gets way overvalued or until I see a change in management execution (for the worse).

data.title: John B. Sanfilippo & Son: This Family Business Is Nuts
data.description:
Expansion Leads To Temporarily Low Free Cash Flow

Small cap stocks can be harder to analyze because individual events like changing purchase patterns at a major customer or large capital projects can have a big temporary impact on financials. However, companies that have repeatedly worked through similar issues in the past often deserve the benefit of the doubt when predicting future performance. That appears to be the case with John B. Sanfilippo & Son (JBSS), a $900 million market cap distributor of nuts, mixes, and snack bars. I have covered JBSS previously on Seeking Alpha from 2020-2022. While I sold the stock in December 2022, it was more to raise cash for personal needs than any out of any concern about the stock. Nevertheless, the stock has not done much since then, still trading below where it was at that time and producing a total return of around 2.1% annualized.

2025 was a particularly tough year for the stock as the price declined along with the total dividend payout, but the lower dividends were for a good reason. JBSS has been reinvesting in its own growth by expanding into the faster-growing snack and nutrition bar market. The company bought a bar manufacturing facility from TreeHouse Foods in fiscal 2024 and increased capital spending in fiscal 2025 and 2026 to further expand bar production capacity. (The company's fiscal year ends in June.)

The temporary reduction in free cash flow could be concerning if only looking at the numbers, but JBSS has come through other periods of high spending in good shape and with continued growth. The company also manages the balance sheet conservatively, avoiding long-term debt except for secured loans such as mortgages, capital leases, or equipment-backed loans. This causes some fluctuation in the special dividends paid to shareholders but avoids excessive leverage and interest expense. To see how this strategy has worked out for the company, a quick review of history is useful.

Welcome To The Family

Despite the current name, JBSS was founded by John B. Sanfilippo and his father, Gaspare, in downtown Chicago in 1922. The business was originally engaged in shelling pecans. After their only customer went out of business in the 1930s, the company diversified into other types of nuts. Over the years, JBSS grew and acquired its own retail brands and distribution while continuing to expand its capabilities to package nuts for third party customers. JBSS also vertically integrated by establishing processing facilities near key nut growing areas. The company progressively moved its headquarters into larger and larger facilities, ending up in the western suburb of Elgin, IL around 2006, just as John's grandsons Jeffrey (CEO) and Jasper (COO) became the fourth generation of Sanfilippos to run the company.

This new headquarters was financed by mortgage debt, and while JBSS continued to do well, the stock was beaten up during the financial crisis, creating many opportunities between 2006 and 2012 to buy below the $12 level where the company came public in 1991.

JBSS resumed its growth in the 2010s, buying several nut and snack mix brands including Orchard Valley Harvest before the latest expansion into snack and nutrition bars.

While this history is interesting on its own, it also shows that JBSS has been successful at deleveraging after a big expansion while delivering volume and profit growth. The mortgage on the Elgin facility is now paid off, with the only long-term debt now consisting of a mortgage on the Selma, TX peanut processing facility and an equipment loan secured by the new bar manufacturing line.

The new capacity is scheduled to be up and running in July 2026, which is the start of Fiscal 2027. With less need for capex once the new line is installed, I expect free cash flow to get back to pre-2024 levels, enabling the equipment loan to be paid off while still allowing dividends to increase.

There are a couple other quirks about JBSS new investors should be aware of. First, it truly is a family business, with the Sanfilippo family holding special Class A shares which have the same economic interest as common shares but 10 times the voting power. Overall, the family has about 75% of the total voting power. They also have the right to elect seven board members, currently composed of Jeffrey, Jasper, and other siblings and cousins. Common shareholders have the right to elect three independent board members. While this close control can be a risk, the family has treated common shareholders well, paying a generous though variable dividend over time, and avoiding excessive compensation. JBSS has not done any buybacks, yet the share count has only grown from 11.2 million to 11.7 million since June 2016 due to share based compensation, a growth rate of just 0.5% per year.

As hinted at earlier, the dividend policy is unusual. JBSS has paid a regular dividend only after the end of each fiscal year since 2017. This payout has increased $0.05 each year and reached $0.90 in 2025. In addition, the company often pays special dividends. This often happens at the same time as the annual regular dividend but also can occur at any time.

Despite the variability, since 2015, the overall dividend yield had been around 4%, although it has been higher in years like 2019-2021 when it was helped first by lower raw nut prices, then by pandemic-induced home snacking and baking demand. That earlier spike made the dropoff in 2024 and 2025 look concerning, but the company was investing the cash in long-term growth with the TreeHouse acquisition and bar production capex.

Capex is again expected to be high in 2026 as JBSS wraps up the bar manufacturing project. Total capex for the year was forecasted to be $104 million, of which $47.3 million was actually spent in the first half. Still, the company appears confident in cash generation, with a $1.50 per share special dividend recently declared. This dividend is payable on May 21 to shareholders who buy the stock by April 24.

Current Challenges And Opportunities

JBSS has improved its margins over the past 10 years by selling a higher percentage of total sales into the consumer channel, which made up 82% of sales in fiscal 2025 compared to 62% in 2015. (Commercial Ingredients and Contract Packaging make up the other two channels.) However, within the consumer channel, JBSS has faced some challenges with its own brands including Fisher and Orchard Valley Harvest, where distribution can sometimes be at the mercy of large retailers. Walmart (WMT) made up 40% of JBSS sales in 2025 and Target (TGT) was another 11%. The company has increased private label sales which include store brands less likely to be displaced by other name brands. The private label business now makes up 83% of consumer channel sales.

Like many consumer products, nuts and bars enjoyed a demand spike during the pandemic for home snacking and baking, followed by a decline in subsequent years. As we see in the company's last shareholder presentation, volumes declined from 2022 to 2024 but appear to have bottomed. Overall category 2025 volumes are above 2024 in both nuts and bars. Nut volumes remain below 2021 levels, however, while bar volumes are higher. This supports the current company strategy to diversify into bars.

Valuation

JBSS has seen steady sales growth since 2021 and net income growth since 2019, but the share price is still trading at 2018 levels.

As a result, the stock's price/sales ratio of 0.8 is similar to where it was in the 2016-2018 time frame. The price/earnings ratio of 13.2, while above 2025 levels, is still cheaper than at any point between 2016 and 2024. These values compare favorably to the Consumer Staples sector averages, which currently are 1.0 on P/S and 19.8 on P/E (both on a trailing 12-month basis).

JBSS currently has just one professional analyst following the stock and issuing earnings estimates. In January, he lowered the FY 2026 earnings per share estimate from $5.94 to $5.57 but raised FY 2027 from $7.14 to $7.31.

The estimate cut for FY 2026 gave JBSS a D+ grade on Earnings Revisions in the Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings, and is a caution flag considering the fiscal H1 actual EPS of $3.12, implying earnings of just $2.45 for the second half. However, the recent special dividend declaration suggests greater company confidence in current conditions. Also, the EPS growth of 31% for FY 2027 is impressive for a packaged food stock and suggests a permanent boost in earnings power from the bar investment. The price target of $109 also still offers upside from current levels.

Quant Ratings also give JBSS a D+ on Valuation, however that could be driven by a low dividend yield that does not reflect the recent special dividend. As the recent special and year-end dividends coming in August get incorporated into the calculation, the Valuation score could improve along with the EPS Revision score, causing JBSS's Quant Score to improve considerably from the current 2.85 in the Hold range.

Conclusion

Compared to the May-October 2025 period when JBSS was trading in the $60-$70 range, it may be a bit later than optimal to call it a Buy. Nevertheless, the improved cash flow driven by the completion of the investment in snack bar capacity is still on the way. Earnings are also set to take a step up in fiscal 2027 as the bar production comes online. This would leave JBSS attractively valued even in the low $80s, still with 30% upside based on the Wall Street price target.

Appendix: Trading Strategy

As a small cap stock, JBSS can trade with low liquidity. The recent special dividend announcement could be causing the price to spike in the short term. I recommend using limit orders and bidding for no more than a couple hundred shares at a time. Alternatively, investors could sell cash-secured puts to buy the stock if it falls below the strike price. Just be aware of wide bid-ask spreads and small position sizes on the puts as well. Also, note that strike prices may be adjusted in the event of a special dividend. For example, I recently reacquired the stock at an effective price of $76.36 by selling puts with a strike price of $78.40. These were originally $80 puts, adjusted for special dividends of $0.60 in August 2025 and $1.00 in December 2025.
